clinical_history: 78-yr-old woman. What would the coronary angiogram show?  What was likely notable about the second heart sound?
diagnosis:
ECG shows sinus rhythm with premature atrial complexes (PACs). Notable findings are right bundle branch block (RBBB) (causing widely, but physiologically split S2) and pathologic Q wave V1-V3, due to underlying anterior/septal myocardial infarction (MI) caused by left anterior descending occlusion, which could be proximal.
The T wave inversions may be due to a combination of primary (ischemia) and secondary factors (RBBB-altered depolarization/repolarization). As a rule, "secondary" T wave inversions ascribable to RBBB alone, are only seen in leads with an RsR' morphology. In this case, the T wave inversions in leads V4  and aVL cannot be explained as "secondary." So in this case, given the findings of pathologic Q waves, the T wave inversions in the antero-lateral leads are consistent with ischemia. Thus, primary and secondary T wave abnormalities may co-exist in the same ECG (or even in the same lead).
